summary: DeVault offers a rare positive assessment of Go, praising its long-term commitment to simplicity, stability, and robustness. He argues Go essentially invented a new niche as an 'Internet programming language' distinct from systems programming. He credits the Go team's disciplined restraint and centralized leadership for producing both an excellent language and an unusually high-quality ecosystem. He contends that Go's insulation from community demands is actually essential to its value, and that the language has earned a rare status as a reliable engineering tool built to last decades.
key_points: Go's greatest strength is its disciplined commitment to simplicity, stability, and robustness — traits DeVault prizes above all others in software design
Go is not a systems programming language but the first widely successful 'Internet programming language', occupying a new niche between systems and high-level languages
Go's simple design has the profound effect of causing its community to produce similarly high-quality, consistent libraries
Go's insulation from community wants is actually advantageous — listening too much would make it a kitchen sink and destroy its value
The Go team's dispassionate, matter-of-fact engineering ethos is essential to maintaining the language's quality
money_quotes: The Go team works with an ethos of careful restraint, with each feature given deliberate consideration towards identifying the simplest and most complete solution.
It's a truly profound achievement to have produced a design which the community at large utilizes to make similarly excellent designs as a basic consequence of the language's simple elegance.
Go is the best-in-class for a new breed of software: an Internet programming language.
If Go listened to the community as much as they want, it would become a kitchen sink, and cease to be interesting to me.
Rather than being closely tied to its community's wants, Go generally does a much better job of being closely tied to its community's needs.
Go has attained an elusive status in the programming canon as a robust engineering tool that can be expected to work, and work well, in its applications for decades to come.

It was a mistake for Go to be marketed as a systems language. Any systems programmer would rightfully tell you that a language with a garbage collector and magic cooperative/pre-emptive threads is a non-starter for systems programming.
key_insight: Go's greatest achievement is not any single feature but its disciplined restraint, which produces simplicity that propagates throughout its entire ecosystem and establishes it as the defining 'Internet programming language.'
